Data warehouse built for the cloud: Data warehouse built for the cloud
Execute a SQL query against your Snowfla: Execute a SQL query against your Snowflake database and return the results as rows.
Execute multiple SQL statements in seque: Execute multiple SQL statements in sequence against your Snowflake database. Optionally wrap them in a transaction so all changes are rolled back if any statement fails.
Insert a row into a table.
Update one or more rows in a Snowflake t: Update one or more rows in a Snowflake table that match a WHERE condition.
Delete one or more rows from a Snowflake: Delete one or more rows from a Snowflake table that match a WHERE condition.
Insert a new row or update an existing o: Insert a new row or update an existing one if a row with the same match column value already exists (MERGE INTO).
Insert multiple rows into a Snowflake ta: Insert multiple rows into a Snowflake table in a single operation.
Retrieve a single row from a Snowflake t: Retrieve a single row from a Snowflake table by matching a column value.
Search for rows in a Snowflake table usi: Search for rows in a Snowflake table using an optional WHERE condition, ordering, and row limit.
Retrieve the column definitions (name, d: Retrieve the column definitions (name, data type, nullability, default) for a Snowflake table.
List all tables in a Snowflake schema.
Call a stored procedure in Snowflake and: Call a stored procedure in Snowflake and return its result.
Create or replace a Snowflake Dynamic Ta: Create or replace a Snowflake Dynamic Table that automatically refreshes based on a query. Dynamic Tables are a declarative way to define a table whose content is derived from a query, updated on a schedule.
Load data into a Snowflake table from a: Load data into a Snowflake table from a named internal or external stage using COPY INTO.
The SQL statement to execute. Use `:1`,: The SQL statement to execute. Use `:1`, `:2`… or `?` as placeholders for the **Parameters** values below to safely pass dynamic values without SQL injection risk.
Values to bind to the placeholders (`:1`: Values to bind to the placeholders (`:1`, `:2`…, or `?`) in the SQL query. Provide them in the same order they appear in the query. Using parameters is the safe way to pass dynamic values — never concatenate user input directly into the SQL.
Maximum time in milliseconds to wait for: Maximum time in milliseconds to wait for the query to complete before cancelling it. Defaults to 30 000 ms (30 seconds).
An optional label sent to Snowflake to i: An optional label sent to Snowflake to identify this client. Visible in query history under **Monitoring → Query History → Client Application**. Useful for auditing which automation triggered a query.
List of SQL statements to run in order. Use `:1`, `:2`… placeholders to reference shared **Parameters** values. Avoid `?` placeholders when running multiple queries — use numbered placeholders instead to prevent unexpected behaviour.
Values shared across all queries for the: Values shared across all queries for the numbered placeholders (`:1`, `:2`…). Provide them in order. Unused parameters are allowed.
When enabled, all queries run inside a s: When enabled, all queries run inside a single transaction. If any query fails, every preceding change in this batch is rolled back automatically.
Maximum time in milliseconds to wait for: Maximum time in milliseconds to wait for each query to complete before cancelling it. Defaults to 30 000 ms (30 seconds).
An optional label sent to Snowflake to i: An optional label sent to Snowflake to identify this client. Visible in query history under **Monitoring → Query History → Client Application**.
Select the Snowflake database to use. Da: Select the Snowflake database to use. Databases are listed under **Data → Databases** in the Snowflake console.
Select the schema within the chosen data: Select the schema within the chosen database. Schemas are listed under **Data → Databases → [your database]** in the Snowflake console.
Select the table to work with.
Enter the value for each column in the n: Enter the value for each column in the new row.
Only filled columns will be updated. Lea: Only filled columns will be updated. Leave a column empty to keep its existing value.
A SQL condition to filter which rows to: A SQL condition to filter which rows to update (e.g. `id = 42` or `status = 'active'`). Leave empty to update ALL rows in the table (use with caution). **Security note:** this value is embedded directly in SQL. Only use static values or data from trusted, internal steps — never pass unvalidated end-user input here.

A SQL condition to filter which rows to delete (e.g. `id = 42` or `status = 'archived'`). This field is required to prevent accidental deletion of all rows. **Security note:** this value is embedded directly in SQL. Only use static values or data from trusted, internal steps — never pass unvalidated end-user input here.
The column used to identify whether a ro: The column used to identify whether a row already exists (e.g. the primary key or a unique identifier column).
One item per row. Each item must be a JS: One item per row. Each item must be a JSON object with column names as keys. Example: `{"name": "Alice", "age": 30}`. When mapping from a previous step, pass the entire array directly.
The column to search in (e.g. the primar: The column to search in (e.g. the primary key or unique identifier column).
The value to look up in the ID column.
Optional SQL WHERE condition to narrow d: Optional SQL WHERE condition to narrow down results (e.g. `status = 'active' AND age > 18`). Leave empty to return all rows (subject to the row limit). **Security note:** this value is embedded directly in SQL. Only use static values or data from trusted, internal steps — never pass unvalidated end-user input here.
Optional column(s) to sort results by (S: Optional column(s) to sort results by (SQL ORDER BY clause). Examples: `created_at DESC` sorts newest first; `name ASC, age DESC` sorts by name then age. **Security note:** this value is embedded directly in SQL. Only use static values or data from trusted, internal steps — never pass unvalidated end-user input here.
Maximum number of rows to return. Defaul: Maximum number of rows to return. Defaults to 100.
Select the stored procedure to call.
Positional arguments to pass to the stor: Positional arguments to pass to the stored procedure in order. Leave empty if the procedure takes no arguments.
The name for the new dynamic table (e.g. `DAILY_SALES_SUMMARY`). Only letters, digits, underscores, and `$` are allowed.
How up-to-date the dynamic table data sh: How up-to-date the dynamic table data should be (called **Target Lag** in Snowflake). Examples: `1 minute`, `5 minutes`, `1 hour`. Use `DOWNSTREAM` to only refresh when a downstream object explicitly requests it.
Select the virtual warehouse to use for: Select the virtual warehouse to use for this operation. Warehouses are listed under **Admin → Warehouses** in the Snowflake console. Leave empty to use the warehouse set in your connection.
The SELECT statement that defines the co: The SELECT statement that defines the content of the dynamic table. Example: `SELECT user_id, SUM(amount) AS total FROM orders GROUP BY user_id`
Path to the Snowflake stage (a staging a: Path to the Snowflake stage (a staging area that holds files before loading). For **internal stages**: use `@stage_name` or `@schema.stage_name/folder/`. For **external stages** (S3, GCS, Azure): use `@schema.stage_name/prefix/`. Example: `@my_stage/data/`. You can find your stage names under **Data → Databases → [your database] → Stages**.
The file format of the data files in the: The file format of the data files in the stage.
Optional regular expression to load only: Optional regular expression to load only files whose names match the pattern (e.g. `.*\.csv` loads only `.csv` files, `sales_2024.*` loads files that start with `sales_2024`). Leave empty to load all files in the stage path.
Number of rows to skip at the top of eac: Number of rows to skip at the top of each CSV file (e.g. `1` to skip a column-header row). Only applies to CSV files. Defaults to 0.
When enabled, the load fails if the numb: When enabled, the load fails if the number of columns in a file does not match the number of columns in the target table. Disable to allow files with fewer columns.
What to do when a row fails validation d: What to do when a row fails validation during loading.
Triggers when a new row is inserted into: Triggers when a new row is inserted into the selected table. Uses a timestamp column you choose to detect rows added since the last check.
Triggers when a row is inserted or updat: Triggers when a row is inserted or updated in the selected table. Uses an "updated at" timestamp column you choose — any row whose timestamp is newer than the last check will fire the trigger.
Triggers when a new column is added to t: Triggers when a new column is added to the selected table. Checked by polling every few minutes.
Triggers when a new table is created in: Triggers when a new table is created in the selected schema. Checked by polling every few minutes.
Triggers when a new view is created in t: Triggers when a new view is created in the selected schema. Checked by polling every few minutes.
Select the column that stores when each: Select the column that stores when each row was inserted (e.g. `CREATED_AT`, `INSERT_TIME`). Only rows with a timestamp in this column newer than the last check will fire the trigger.

Select the column that stores when each row was last modified (e.g. `UPDATED_AT`, `LAST_MODIFIED_TIME`). Both new rows and rows updated after the last check will fire the trigger. The column must be updated by your application or a DEFAULT clause every time a row changes.
